title: A Structured Psychiatric Intervention for Cancer Patients
abstract:
A Structured Psychiatric
Intervention for Cancer Patients
I. Changes Over Time in Methods of Coping and Affective Disturbance
Fawzy I. Fawzy, MD; Norman Cousins; Nancy W. Fawzy, RN, MN; Margaret E. Kemeny, PhD;
Robert Elashoff, PhD; Donald Morton, MD
\s=b\We evaluated the immediate and long-term effects on psy-
chological distress and coping methods of a 6-week, structured,
psychiatric group intervention for postsurgical patients with ma-
lignant melanoma. The intervention consisted of health educa-
tion, enhancement of problem-solving skills, stress manage-
ment (eg, relaxation techniques), and psychological support. In
spite of good prognosis, most patients had high levels of psycho-
logical distress at baseline, comparable with other patients with
cancer. However, at the end of brief psychiatric intervention, the
experimental subjects (n = 38), while not without some distress,
exhibited higher vigor and greater use of active-behavioral cop-
ing than the controls (n = 28). At 6 months' follow-up, the group
differences were even more pronounced. The intervention-group
patients then showed significantly lower depression, fatigue,
confusion, and total mood disturbance as well as higher vigor.
They were also using significantly more active-behavioral and
active-cognitive coping than the controls. These results indicate
that a short-term psychiatric group intervention for patients with
malignant melanoma effectively reduces psychological distress
and enhances longer-term effective coping.
(Arch Gen Psychiatry. 1990;47:720-725)
Three
of every four families in the United States can
expect to have at least one member in whom cancer
develops.1 Specific psychological disturbances in response to
the diagnosis of advanced cancer have been documented.2"18
Patients with early diagnoses of cancer with good prognosis,
whether or not they objectively have "little or nothing to
worry about," still experience psychological distress; some
may even be in a state of crisis.19 Identifying the kinds of
psychological reactions experienced and coping methods used
as well as establishing effective interventions is useful to
improve quality of life and enhance utilization of treatment
regimens that may improve both quality and prolongation of
life. Although the field of psycho-oncology has extensively
investigated patients with advanced stages of cancer,2'18 stud¬
ies ofpatients with early stages of cancer and good prognoses
are yet to be done.
Accepted for publication March 22,1990.
From the Departments ofPsychiatry (Drs Fawzy and Kemeny, Mr Cousins,
and Ms Fawzy), Microbiology and Immunology (Dr Kemeny), and Biomathe-
matics (Dr Elashoff), the Dean's Office (Mr Cousins), the John Wayne Cancer
Clinic/Division ofSurgical Oncology (Dr Morton), and the Jonsson Comprehen-
sive Cancer Center (Drs Fawzy, Elashoff, and Morton and Mr Cousins), UCLA
School of Medicine.
Reprint requests to UCLA/Neuropsychiatric Institute, 760 Westwood Pla-
za, Los Angeles, CA 90024-175919 (Dr Fawzy).
The coping methods used by patients to deal with their
cancer have become a focus for systematic inquiry.20,21 Three
general coping methods have been described: active-behav¬
ioral coping, active-cognitive coping, and avoidance coping.22
These methods have also been factor analyzed into eight
strategies that provide a convenient framework for classify¬
ing coping reactions28 (further described in the "Subjects and
Methods" section).
Most ofthese methods and strategies show differing associ¬
ations with psychological distress. In general, patients who
use active-behavioral and active-cognitive coping methods
report more positive affective states, higher levels of self-
esteem, and fewer physical symptoms.21"27 A positive associa¬
tion has been noted between avoidance coping (avoidance,
denial, and escape) and psychological distress such as anxi¬
ety, indirectly expressed anger, depression, and lower quali¬
ty of life.22,23 These studies indicate that active-behavioral
coping and active-cognitive coping rather than avoidance cop¬
ing may be quite helpful psychologically.9,28"81
A number of therapeutic interventions (eg, individual
psychotherapy, group therapy, psychoanalysis, hypnosis,
guided imagery, education, relaxation training, and self-help
groups32"37) have been implemented to deal with the psycholog¬
ical reactions ofpatients with cancer. So far, the literature on
group therapy with cancer patients remains largely descrip¬
tive, and few studies have investigated long-term effective¬
ness. Such group interventions have been credited with help¬
ing patients in a number of ways, eg, improvement of "self-
concept and adjustment to illness,"38 decline ofnegative affect
(anxiety, hostility, and depression),34 sharing of problem-
solving skills and hope,39 and ventilation of emotions.40
Tb our knowledge, only one prospective, group-interven¬
tion outcome study has been reported. Spiegel and col¬
leagues30 studied patients with metastatic breast cancer and
relatively poor prognoses. These patients met weekly in psy¬
chological support groups for up to a year. Assessment of
mood states, maladaptive coping responses (eg, overeating,
overdrinking, and increased smoking), phobias, denial, locus
of control, and self-esteem were completed every 4 months.
At 12 months, patients who participated in the support group
showed significantly less tension, less fatigue, less confusion,
and more vigor than those in the control group. They also
showed a trend toward having less depression, fewer malad¬
justed coping responses, and fewer phobias. At 10-year fol¬
low-up only 3 of the patients were alive, and death records
were obtained for the other 83. Survival from time ofrandom-
Downloaded From: http://archpsyc.jamanetwork.com/ by a Monash University Library User  on 06/17/2015
 ization and onset of the intervention was, somewhat to the
authors' surprise, significantly different, with a mean ( ± SD)
of 36.3 ±37.6 months in the intervention group compared
with 18.9 ±10.8 months in the control group. Lower mood
disturbance and higher ratings ofvigor on the Profile ofMood
States (POMS) at the end of the intervention period were
significantly associated with greater longevity.81
The present study represents a prospective, longitudinal
investigation of patients with early diagnoses of malignant
melanoma with good prognosis. It studies the effects of a
structured, short-term psychiatric group intervention con¬
sisting of health education, stress management (eg, relax¬
ation training), illness-related problem-solving skills, and
psychological support on levels of psychological distress, use
of effective coping (active-behavioral and active-cognitive
methods, as well as active-positive, active-expressive, active-
reliance, cognitive-positive, and distraction strategies), and
use of ineffective coping (cognitive-passive and passive-resig¬
nation strategies). These effects are examined at the end of
the intervention as well as at 6 months' follow-up. The project
was designed to study psychobehavioral factors and to accom¬
modate
a battery of immune
cell
measures (reported
separately41).
SUBJECTS AND METHODS
Selection Criteria
We screened every patient with malignant melanoma referred to
the John Wayne Cancer Clinic, UCLA, from August 1,1985, through
August 31, 1986. Patients were deemed eligible for the study if
(1) their illness had been recently diagnosed as stage I (no metastasis)
or II (local node metastasis), ie, patients with good prognoses;
(2) their medical intervention required only excision of the primary
tumor and any metastatic nodes; (3) they had not undergone previous
psychiatric treatment; (4) they were at least 18 years old; and (5) they
were able to read and speak English. Patients were excluded if they
were undergoing immunotherapy (eg, regimens of BCG vaccine,
tumor cell vaccine, interferon, or interleukin), chemotherapy, or
radiotherapy or if they were receiving medication (eg, steroids or
large doses of aspirin) that could affect their immunological func¬
tion,42 since immune cell measures were a part ofthe study (reported
elsewhere41).
With permission from each patient's physician, the patients were
interviewed and the study was described to them. They were told
that we were interested in assessing how they were coping with their
diagnosis and if a structured group intervention might benefit pa¬
tients with cancer. They would be randomly assigned to either the
intervention group or the control group if they chose to participate.
The control group would receive only the psychological and immuno¬
logical assessments. They would not receive any form of psychologi¬
cal or educational intervention. Eighty patients agreed to participate
and signed informed consents. Twelve patients refused, citing the
time or distance involved as the main reasons. Data collection and
intervention were conducted in four consecutive waves, each approx¬
imately 4 weeks apart. A new group was started as soon as 20 patients
were recruited. Of the 40 patients originally assigned to the experi¬
mental group, 2 (5%) were excluded from the analysis. One of these
was a patient who died early in the intervention period. In the first
group session, one of us (F.I.F) clinically assessed each subject for
the presence of major depression according to DSM-III-R criteria.
Only 1 ofthe above subjects met those criteria; he was treated with an
antidepressant and excluded from the data analysis (final  = 38). Of
the 40 patients originally assigned to the control group, 10 (25%)
dropped out before the start of the project when informed that they
were assigned to the control group even though they had already
been informed ofthis possibility, and 2 (5%) had incomplete data (final
 = 28). The dropouts were not significantly different in age, sex,
degree ofdisease, or any other demographic variable from those who
continued to participate.
Assessment
The psychological data were collected from the intervention-group
patients before the intervention (baseline), at 6 weeks (immediately
after intervention), and at 6 months after intervention, at the site of
the intervention. The control patients completed their questionnaires
at home at the same times. Their questionnaires were sent through
the mail to coincide with the assessment points of the intervention
group.
The data included personality profiles, affective state, quality of
life, coping styles, social support, family environment, psychological
adjustment to illness, and immunological measures. In this article,
we will describe the results pertaining to affective states and coping
styles. Future articles will deal with the remaining information.
Affective states and coping style were obtained from the POMS43
and the Dealing With Illness-Coping Inventory.23 The POMS is a 65-
item instrument that measures tension-anxiety, depression-dejec¬
tion, anger-hostility, vigor-activity, fatigue-inertia, and confusion-
bewilderment. (For this study the vigor-activity scale was modified
so that this variable reflects lack ofvigor and is thus consistent with
the negative connotation of the other scales.) The results can be
summed to generate a total mood disturbance (TMD) score for affec¬
tive state. High POMS scores indicate high levels of mood distur¬
bance for the 7 days preceding assessment. Previous studies of
patients with acquired immunodeficiency syndrome (AIDS) or cancer
have shown the POMS scales to be valid and reliable
mea-
„,,„„„ 3,23,30,43-47
sures.
The Dealing With Illness-Coping Inventory is a 48-item inventory
that assesses current cognitive and behavioral responses made in
efforts to cope with illness. Responses are categorized into three
general coping methods: (1) the active-behavioral method, in which
the patient tries to change some aspect of the illness by such active
means as exercising, use of relaxation techniques, and frequent
consultations with his or her physician; (2) the active-cognitive meth¬
od, in which the patient tries to understand the illness and accepts its
effect on life by focusing on positive changes that have occurred since
the onset ofillness; and (3) the avoidance method, in which the patient
avoids others, hides his or her feelings about the illness, and refuses
to think about the illness. The data are then further analyzed as eight
specific coping strategies: (1) active-positive strategy (increasing
involvement, planning action, enjoying life "one day at a time");
(2) active-expressive strategy (talking with others to gain informa¬
tion or to offer support to others with cancer); (3) active-reliance
strategy (seeking a friend or relative for help or a physician for
intervention); (4) cognitive-positive strategy (understanding the ill¬
ness, creating meaning, thinking about positive changes); (5) cogni¬
tive-passive strategy (ruminating, daydreaming); (6) avoidance-soli¬
tary strategy (avoiding others, taking drugs, or smoking more than
usual); (7) distraction strategy (going out more socially, doing some¬
thing nice for oneself); and (8) passive-resignation strategy (prepar¬
ing for "the worst," keeping feelings secret from others). The higher
the score on these subscales, the greater the use of a particular coping
method. A previous study of patients with AIDS has shown the
Dealing With Illness scales to be useful and internally consistent.23
Demographic information was obtained directly from the patients
or from their charts at baseline.
Treatment
Both intervention and control patients underwent standard surgi¬
cal treatment of their tumors, consisting of wide excision of the
primary site and regional lymphadenectomy when indicated. They
also completed the same schedule ofassessments. The patients in the
control group did not receive our psychological or educational inter¬
vention and had no contact with the intervention-group leaders.
The intervention-group patients participated in the structured
group intervention. Groups of 7 to 10 patients met for IV2 hours
weekly for 6 weeks. The group meetings, which were structured yet
supportive, were led by two of us (F.I.F. and N.C.). The model
consisted of four components: (1) health education; (2) enhancement
of illness-related problem-solving skills; (3) stress management (eg,
relaxation techniques); and (4) psychological support.
The health education component consisted of information about
health care promotion and maintenance, especially in regard to can¬
cer prevention (nutrition, exercise, and how to deal with the hazards
of sun exposure by use of sunblocks, hats, etc). The most important
result of health education was to allow the patients to continue to
participate in their favorite daily activities in a healthy and safe
manner.
The problem-solving techniques used were modeled after those of

The problem-solving techniques used were modeled after those of
Project Omega,3,4S which emphasizes the reality that cancer diagnosis
often initiates an extended period of uncertainty, disability, and
distress. It also highlights the specific problems that patients with
cancer particularly encounter and utilizes the teaching of positive
coping strategies as a way of diminishing stress and enhancing cop¬
ing. It includes learning an approach to problem solving, practicing
the process in the group, applying the approach to personal problems,
and discussing the resolution of the problems. A series of pictures
illustrating common dilemmas in cancer are shown to the patients to
initiate discussions that often lead to effective resolution of the prob¬
lems. The illustrated situations include loneliness and isolation, fear
and apprehension, morale maintenance with social support, physi¬
cian-patient relationships, changes in body image, sexuality and
personal contact, general mood states, communication, social alien¬
Stress management techniques included teaching the patients how
to assess their own sources and levels of stress as well as several
relaxation techniques.30,38,45
Psychological support from the staff was inherent throughout the
intervention and was initiated with a series of talks on engendering
hope and determination and mobilizing coping resources. There was
also a great deal of within-group support provided by the patients
themselves. Members discussed many topics, including illness-re¬
lated concerns, family problems, and communication with physi-
A linear repeated-measures covariance model was used to compare
the POMS and coping scales between the intervention and control
groups. Through residual plots, a model was obtained that took the
following formfor all six scales at 6 weeks (T^ and 6 months (T2): Scale
score at   or T2 = scale score at baseline + quadratic polynomial in
age + treatment + homoscedastic score. Since there were signifi¬
cant differences at baseline between the groups in certain POMS and
coping subscale scores and age, this model controlled for baseline
POMS and coping scores as well as age. A similar linear covariance
model was used to compare the coping methods and strategies used
At the start ofthe study, the demographic and clinical variables did
not differ significantly between the intervention and control groups,
with the exception of the younger age of the control group (interven¬
tion group mean, 45.5 years, vs control group mean, 37.64 years;
Ps.0062, Student's t test). (Age was statistically controlled for in the
analyses ofintervention effects.) The mean age for the entire sample
was 42 years; 53% were female, 47% were male; 99% of the sample
was white; 75% had college or education above; 64% were married;
The baseline POMS scores indicated that the study patients as a
whole had high levels of psychological distress, comparable with
those of other types of patients with cancer47 as well as patients
suffering from AIDS46 or undergoing bone marrow transplanta¬
Intervention Effects on Affective States
The POMS scores obtained at 6 weeks, immediately following the
intervention, indicated that the only significant difference between
the two groups was on the vigor scale ( «.026). Specifically, the
intervention-group patients reported higher levels of vigor. In addi¬
tion, there were trends suggesting lower levels of anxiety, confusion,
depression, fatigue, and TMD in the intervention group (Table 2).
The 6-month POMS scores showed that the differences between the
groups had increased in magnitude. The intervention group now
showed significantly less depression-dejection ( «.017), fatigue-in¬
confusion-bewilderment ( «.013),
(P=s.006), while showing significantly more vigor-activity (P=s.001)
Intervention Effects on Coping Methods
Immediately after the 6-week intervention, the Dealing With Ill¬
ness-Coping Inventory demonstrated that the intervention group
. — Profile of Mood States Scores: Population
*AIDS indicates acquired immunodeficiency syndrome; TMD, total mood
disturbance. Values are mean ± SD.
used active-behavioral (Pss.0001) coping methods significantly more
often than did the control group. The intervention group was also
using significantly more of the following coping strategies: active-
(P«.002), cognitive-positive (P=s.036), and distraction (P=s.008) (Ta¬

coping methods than the control group (Table 3). The intervention
group continued to use significantly more of the same coping strate¬

often than the control group (P*.003) (Table 3).
Correlations Among Three Coping Methods
For the intervention-group patients, at baseline, the avoidance
coping method was significantly and positively correlated with anxi¬
ety (r= .45, P=s.005), depression(r= .46, P=s.005), confusion(r = .48,
Ps.003), lack of vigor (r = .40, P=s.014), anger (r = .42, Pss.010), and
TMD (r=.47, P=s.003), while active-behavioral coping was signifi¬
cantly and negatively correlated with anger (r = .36, P=s.028). Imme¬
diately after the intervention, these correlations continued; in addi¬
correlation with lack ofvigor (r=.33, P=s.040). At6months, all these
correlations remained significant, and in addition, active-behavioral
coping showed a significant negative correlation with fatigue (r =.33,
P=s.045) and TMD (r = .39, Pss.015), and active-cognitive coping was
significantly negatively correlated with anger (r= .35, P=s.034) (Ta¬
Despite the prevailing beliefthat patients with early stages
of cancer and good prognoses should have "nothing to worry
about," it is apparent that patients with newly diagnosed
cancer, regardless of their prognosis, exhibit symptoms of
psychological distress similar to those ofpatients undergoing
bone marrow transplantation as a last-resort treatment for
leukemia,4449 patients with AIDS,4448 and other patients with
cancer in general.47 Apparently, these patients, who are oth¬
erwise high-functioning and healthy, are still dealing with the
immediate stress of being diagnosed as having a possible life-
The diagnosis of cancer immediately and prematurely im¬
poses a perceived disruption in the "life trajectory" of the
patient. Irrespective ofhis or her age or phase in the life cycle,
the patient is generally concerned with the fear of death;
morbidity associated with treatments; fear of recurrence;
fear ofbecoming dependent or a burden; fear ofabandonment;
fear ofloss offunction, social value, or financial independence;
and inability ultimately to complete the goals oftheir assump-

were analyzed by analysis of covariance with adjusted treatment means.

lIAssumption for equality of slopes not met.
tive world.12 These concerns cause varying degrees ofpsycho¬
logical distress and adjustment problems (coping) that can be
expressed in psychobehavioral terms.
With respect to distress, we noted a state of"psychological
turmoil" that consisted of various elements clearly defined
and documented by the POMS scales. The first of these
elements was confusion-bewilderment, characterized by the
inability to concentrate, uncertainty, muddled ideas, and in¬
efficiency. Second, these patients demonstrated a lack of
vigor, ie, they were less lively and less active. Third, they
exhibited fatigue-inertia, characterized by being worn out,
fatigued, exhausted, and sluggish. They also showed tension-
anxiety by being tense, shaky, edgy, and nervous. Some
expressed anger-hostility in several forms, such as rebellious¬
ness, resentfulness, and bitterness. Finally, they exhibited
depression-dejection, consisting mainly ofsadness, hopeless¬
ness, helplessness, and gloominess. This state of depression-
dejection is a common response to a life-threatening disease
and should not be confused with the DSM-III-R syndrome of
major depression and then be inappropriately treated.
Table 4.—Correlations Between Three Coping Methods and
Mood Disturbance (POMS) at Baseline, 6 Weeks, and
6 Months for Intervention Group*
*POMS indicates Profile of Mood States; TMD, total mood disturbance.
Previous research has focused on the symptoms of depres¬
sion and anxiety as the main psychological difficulties of pa¬
tients with cancer. This narrow focus risks missing other
important measures that can more accurately document the
difficulties that confront these patients. Since a fair number of
patients with cancer are unable or unwilling to admit either
depressed or anxious feelings, they often fail to receive any
help or support from family, friends, or health professionals.
In contrast, our study highlighted the importance of measur¬
ing distress in psychobehavioral terms that reflect functional
ability, involvement in daily activities, and cognitive func¬
tioning. In response to the question of how cancer has had an
impact on their lives, patients are more likely to report that it
has affected their family and social lives, their work situa¬
tions, and their ability to enjoy and get satisfaction from
hobbies or leisure activities rather than reporting feelings of
sadness or anxiousness. They might even express that since
their diagnosis they are confused, bewildered, and less effi¬
cient. Patients may be far more aware of these negative
changes in their daily lives than of any concomitant state of
clinical depression or anxiety.
Six months following the intervention, the intervention
group showed less psychological turmoil than the control
group, as indicated by significantly lower levels of confusion-
bewilderment, depression-dejection, fatigue-inertia,
TMD and higher levels of vigor-activity. Thus, participating
in this intervention appeared to reduce the turmoil associated
with the diagnosis ofcancer and had a long-term impact on the
psychological state of these patients with cancer. Immediate¬
ly following this 6-week intervention, these changes in affect
were beginning to appear, but, interestingly, differences be¬
tween the groups were not yet significant. Affective distress
With respect to patients' psychological difficulties in deal¬
ing with the diagnosis of cancer, we tailored our intervention
to enhance effective coping by emphasizing the importance of
patients' understanding and involvement in the overall
scheme oftheir medical care. This was accomplished by focus¬
ing the educational part of the intervention on the following
areas: learning about the disease; learning to cope with daily
health problems; learning to express feelings; learning "to
live life to its fullest"; and learning about helpful resources.
Relaxation training techniques were also used as an adjuvant
to help patients manage stress encountered in their daily
activities. Finally, the support inherent in the group process
allowed patients to address many oftheir common problems.
Assessment of the patients' coping abilities in contrast to
their affective state revealed a prompt and sustained re¬
sponse in the intervention group. At 6 weeks, the changes
were already apparent, with the intervention-group patients
using significantly more active-behavioral coping methods.
By 6 months, they were also using significantly more active-
cognitive coping as well. They did not reduce their overall use
of avoidance-coping methods. Avoidance, however, may be
differentiated into two components. The first is "distraction,"
which can be adaptive and encompasses going out more social¬
ly, trying to avoid becoming bothered or upset, or doing
something special for oneself. In previous studies, patients
who used distraction had fewer concerns over health, less
mood disturbance, lower anxiety, more vigor, and less fa¬
tigue. They perceived themselves as healthier than patients
who did not use this strategy and actually had fewer medical
symptoms.22,28 The second component of avoidance can be
maladaptive. This includes passive resignation (eg, prepare
for the worst) and avoidance-solitary behaviors (eg, avoid
In our study, the intervention-group patients did increase
their use of distraction and decreased their use of passive
resignation. Their use of effective coping began at 6 weeks,
then continued and increased throughout the following 6
months. These changes could be attributable to the focus on
active coping in the group intervention; however, they might
also have been due to different components ofthe experiment,
such as meeting with other patients with cancer and sharing
experiences. We plan to investigate this further to identify
the critical components of this structured intervention. Fur¬
thermore, the intervention-group patients were advised that
their participation in the structured intervention might bene¬
fit them. Such information and expectation alone may have
The data also show a clear pattern of relationship between
coping methods and mood disturbances as measured by the
POMS. Patients using avoidance-coping had significantly
higher levels of mood disturbance at baseline, 6 weeks, and
6 months. In particular, those patients engaging in the avoid¬
ance-coping method (eg, took drugs or alcohol, avoided others
and kept feelings secret, prepared for the worst and did
nothing) had higher levels of anxiety, depression, confusion,
lack ofvigor, anger, and TMD. In addition, patients who used
distraction were less angry, and patients who used passive
resignation were more anxious. Conversely, the use of active
positive coping methods (eg, understand their illness and
create meaning, increase involvement in their care, talk to
others to gain information/support) were associated with
higher vigor and lower fatigue, anger, and TMD. In summa-
ry, as the use of positive, active coping methods and distrac¬
tion strategies increases, negative mood state decreases.
The study of Spiegel et al30 also demonstrated effective
psychobehavioral results with metastatic breast cancer, but
it involved a much longer (1-year) intervention period than
our 6-week intervention. Such a long-term intervention is
probably most useful in advanced cancer. In our study, the
pragmatic intervention was structured and time limited (ap¬
proximately 9 hours). It can be implemented in research
studies and/or clinical care areas to promote illness-related
problem-solving skills, increased participation in decision
making, and active coping strategies rather than passive
acceptance of the illness. It must be emphasized that this
approach in cases of cancer should only be used as an adjunct
to competent and comprehensive medical care and is not to be
construed as an independent treatment for cancer.
Finally, while other studies have dealt mainly with patients
with advanced disease and poor prognosis, our study, to our
knowledge, is the first to report on patients with early stage
cancer and good prognoses. These patients are part of a
rapidly growing group of individuals who must learn to live
with having had cancer, and they are ideal subjects to benefit

logy Task Force, chaired by Mr Cousins.
